Proposal title: Current Status of ChOWDER: A Web-based Scalable Display for Ultra-High Resolution Web3DGIS
Abstract: ChOWDER is an open-source tiled display system that creates large pixel spaces via distributed web rendering. Building on our high-resolution 3DWebGIS technology presented at FOSS4G 2025, this poster provides an overview and introduces a new feature: GIS overlay visualization for large-scale climate simulation results.
Description:
The scalable display system ChOWDER, is an open-source tiled display system jointly developed by RIKEN and Kyushu University. By arranging multiple display which shows maximized web browser windows in a tiled layout, a single large pixel space can be created, enabling the simultaneous display of various content, including images, text, videos, webcam footage, and even screen sharing from the user's PC.

No special hardware is required to implement this feature; it even works by tiling iPads or Android tablets as display devices.

In 2020, we released a distributed rendering function for 3DWebGIS. This function enables ultra-high-resolution 3DWebGIS display, which is not feasible on a standard PC's multiple displays, and was developed to visualize large-scale weather satellite data while preserving the original data resolution as much as possible. It uses the open-source 3DWebGIS iTowns as a middleware. This achievement was presented in the FOSS4G 2025 Academic Track under the title "Research on the Display of Ultra-Large Point Cloud Data Using a 3DWebGIS Distributed Rendering System."

This poster will provide an overview of ChOWDER's 3D WebGIS distributed rendering function, explain its existing use cases, and introduce a new function for GIS overlay visualization of large-scale climate simulation results that has been under development since the above presentation.

Proposal title: Multimodal AI-based Platform for Damage Assessment, Prediction, and Maintenance of Essential Buildings and Infrastructures in Climate Change
Abstract: We present an integrated multi-modal AI platform designed to transition infrastructure maintenance from "aging-focused" to "climate-proactive." By leveraging GIS-based frameworks, we've developed a foundation for predicting and diagnosing climate-driven structural damage through spatial data and advanced AI modules.
Description:
How do we protect our cities from climate-driven disasters? This lightning talk showcases the first-year results of a multi-modal AI platform development.

While traditional maintenance reacts to structural aging, our system proactively manages climate vulnerability. We successfully designed a spatial-information framework using PostGIS, GeoServer, and OpenLayers, creating functional modules for real-time data collection and visualization.

This session will briefly highlight:
- The core architecture for climate-responsive decision-making.
- Strategies for integrating operational and analytical data.
- Future applications for smart city resilience.

Witness how AI and GIS are reshaping the future of facility safety.

Proposal title: GeoNode usage in Brazil: State of art, challenges, and business opportunities
Abstract: This work aims to discuss, based on real data, a general scenario regarding the current use of GeoNode in Brazil.
Description:
In recent years, a remarkable growth of Brazilian Governmental Institutions that adopted GeoNode to manage geospatial data, replacing proprietary platforms. This aims to present and discuss the use of GeoNode in Brazil, as a Spatial Data Infrastructure (SDI).

The Brazilian government has public policies promoting Free Software, in general. In 2007, a set of laws and regulations was created to establish the National Spatial Data Infrastructure (INDE).

The GeoNode Pioneers, in early 2010s, are the Brazilian Agricultural Research Corporation (EMBRAPA), with GeoInfo, and the Federal Police (PF), using the InteliGeo system - with their own development - Important to emphasize that almost all FOSS4G specialists are government agents or academics.

The methodology involves a creation of a 38-question survey, which was made available between February and March 2026. The questions covered topics ranging from constructive aspects of the SDI, to the size and capabilities of IT teams, as well as general impressions of the platform. The form was sent to the platform managers.

As of March, 2026, 17 SDIs were catalogued, and almost all belong to the government itself, with more than half belonging to Federal’s. There is low participation at the municipal level. Most of the instances are in production and public.

Except for the pioneers, there has been significant growth in the use of GeoNode since 2023, still in the transition between versions 3 and 4, whose predominant. Recent installations are adopting version 5. The preferred installation method is via GeoNode Project, due to HTML templates customization and unsuccessful experiences with modifications to the original code.

Installations are predominantly Docker standalone. A few cases use Kubernetes-based platforms, such as the Geological Survey of Brazil and GEOBASES. This shows preference for containers, but impacts directly on horizontal scalability. Despite good support, GeoServer is the main obstacle to the full scalability of GeoNode.

Regarding GeoServer, it's crucial to modify the image, with splitted administration, REST, and GeoServices, or adapt GeoServer Cloud with the GeoNode configuration to resolve the issue.

Regarding the GIS analyst teams, except for cases of pioneers, data is published by a restricted team. As for the IT teams, they are usually small teams, but with considerable experience in the programming languages ​​used, and few with specific knowledge of the frameworks employed.

Despite this perceived growth, GeoNode doesn't have an official PT-BR translation since version 3. Furthermore, even with significant improvements to the installation process, difficulties are still reported.

Therefore, it's safe to say that there is a technical demand, especially regarding frontend customization, horizontal scalability with Docker Swarm and Kubernetes, as well as training in usage, administration, and template customization.

In terms of business opportunities in Brazil, there is a very scarce number of professionals with knowledge in GeoNode technology, not to mention the legislative barriers that practically prohibit the hiring of development and consulting services. A reasonable alternative would be to establish partnerships with Brazilian companies to participate in government tenders or appoint local representatives, provided they are not public officials.

Proposal title: Establishing an Automated 3D Geospatial Data Pipeline for a National Digital Land Platform
Abstract: This presentation introduces an automated 3D geospatial data pipeline developed for a national Digital Land program. The system integrates large-scale CityGML datasets with an operational validation framework to improve automation, interoperability, and data traceability within national geospatial infrastructures.
Description:
The realization of a hyper-connected Digital National Land requires an integrated operational framework capable of reliably collecting, processing, linking, and providing large-scale heterogeneous 3D geospatial data. Integrating massive CityGML 3.0 datasets with administrative, statistical, and built-environment data (such as BIM and CAD) demands not only data conversion techniques but also automated processing pipelines and operational validation mechanisms.

This presentation introduces an automated 3D geospatial data pipeline and integrated validation framework developed as part of a national Digital National Land program. The system is designed as a modular API-based architecture supporting end-to-end automated processing of large-scale CityGML datasets.

The automated workflow includes:

• Streaming-based parsing for large 3D datasets  
• Coordinate transformation and geometry optimization  
• Automated quality validation and metadata extraction  
• Catalog registration and service integration  
• Data lineage management and version tracking  

Each module operates as an independent service, enabling scalable deployment and flexible operation. The system was implemented within a virtualized infrastructure environment and connected to a National Spatial Information Platform to verify interoperability at a national scale.

Through the integrated pipeline, approximately 92% of data processing tasks were automated while improving traceability through structured data lineage management. The framework demonstrates how research成果 can be extended into sustainable operational environments and provides practical strategies for automation, modularization, and interoperability in national geospatial infrastructures.

Proposal title: From Maps to Operational Infrastructure: An Open Geospatial Digital Twin Framework for Decentralized Wastewater Treatment
Abstract: This talk presents an open geospatial digital twin framework for decentralized wastewater treatment systems. Built on PostgreSQL/PostGIS, OGC APIs, and 3D visualization, the framework integrates sensor data, environmental context, and scenario analysis to support spatially informed monitoring and operational optimization.
Description:
﻿Decentralized wastewater treatment systems are gaining importance under climate variability, urban expansion, and pressure on centralized infrastructure. Unlike large-scale plants, decentralized facilities are spatially distributed and operate under diverse conditions. Optimizing such systems requires not only engineering process models but also spatially integrated digital infrastructures to contextualize performance.
This contribution presents the architectural design of an open geospatial digital twin framework developed during the first-year phase of a project focused on decentralized Microalgae–Membrane Photobioreactor (MPBR) systems. Rather than treating the digital twin as an isolated simulation tool, the framework situates it within an interoperable spatial data infrastructure aligned with FOSS4G principles.
At the core of the design is a PostgreSQL/PostGIS spatial database managing operational and environmental data. Sensor streams—including nutrient concentration, flow rate, dissolved oxygen, temperature, and biomass indicators—are linked to geospatial entities representing decentralized treatment facilities, enabling each module to function as a spatially anchored digital object.
To ensure openness and interoperability, the framework adopts OGC API standards for data exposure and integration. Operational and environmental layers are accessible through standards-based services, allowing facilities to be analyzed within broader spatial ecosystems such as watershed boundaries, land use, and climatic zones.
Three-dimensional representation is designed using CesiumJS and 3D Tiles to visualize treatment modules within their environmental context. This spatial integration supports cross-site comparison, climate-adaptive operational analysis, and temporal performance evaluation.
The framework also outlines a scenario-based analysis structure in which simulated outputs—such as nutrient recovery efficiency under varying inflow or temperature conditions—can be stored as georeferenced temporal datasets. This enables structured benchmarking across decentralized sites operating under different climatic regimes.
To enhance accessibility, a large language model is considered as a supplementary interaction layer that translates natural-language queries into structured spatial filters within the PostGIS environment. Analytical processing remains grounded in the geospatial infrastructure, ensuring transparency and adherence to open standards.
For the FOSS4G community, this work demonstrates how open geospatial technologies can extend beyond visualization into the operational layer of environmental infrastructure systems. By grounding digital twin architectures in interoperable spatial standards, decentralized wastewater systems can be monitored, compared, and optimized within a unified spatial framework.
As decentralized infrastructure expands under circular economy and climate adaptation strategies, spatial interoperability will be essential. The presented framework proposes a replicable design for integrating sensor data, simulation environments, and three-dimensional geospatial visualization into an open and scalable ecosystem.
This work was supported by the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT) of the Republic of Korea and the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) (Grant No. RS-2025-25460186).

Proposal title: Accuracy Verification of Three-Dimensional Models Around the Tottori Sand Dunes Created from Historical Aerial Photographs
Abstract: The area surrounding the Tottori Sand Dunes exhibits diverse land use and environmental changes. To reconstruct these changes, this study used aerial photographs taken periodically by Tottori Prefecture over more than 50 years to evaluate the accuracy of 3D models, and examined whether super-resolution processing contributes to accuracy improvement.
Description:
The land use of the area surrounding the Tottori Sand Dunes has changed alongside human activity. Quantitative assessments of sediment transport volume and topographic change associated with these changes remain scarce. Therefore, this study used aerial photographs taken by Tottori Prefecture at five-year intervals since 1968 to construct 3D models using SfM/MVS, and evaluated their positional accuracy. Additionally, the study examined whether super-resolution processing could improve accuracy.
This study used aerial photographs taken at five-year intervals since 1968. OpenDroneMap was used for 3D model construction, along with proprietary software for comparison.
Accuracy verification results showed that precision equivalent to approximately 1/25,000 under Japanese surveying standards was achieved. Super-resolution processing improved accuracy in cases where the original accuracy was poor; however, when good accuracy had already been achieved prior to super-resolution processing, no significant change was observed. Furthermore, artifacts were sometimes found when super-resolution was applied, indicating that careful consideration is required for practical use.

Proposal title: Modernizing Earth Observation Access: The GEODES Shift to Open Source and Cloud-Optimized Architectures
Abstract: This poster covers our experience with the migration of **GEODES** (the Earth observation catalog of the French space agency, CNES) from proprietary systems to an Open Source, cloud-optimized architecture, highlighting the strategic 'how' and 'why' behind the shift.
Description:
The French space agency (**CNES**) has developed **GEODES**, an innovative Earth Observation catalogue that leverages open-source technologies in order to simplify access to geospatial data and offer advanced services to end-users.

GEODES offers a wide range of open EO data and advanced products originating from CNES missions and from ESA'S collaborative ground segment initiative, as well as fully integrated processing chains providing on-demand products generation. As a free service dedicated to the global community, including researchers, developers, policymakers and enthusiasts alike, GEODES covers various non-EO-specific activities. 

The GEODES portal (geodes-portal.cnes.fr) has launched in November 2024, providing access to a wide range of EO data and advanced products from satellites such as Sentinel, Landsat, Venus and SPOT. At the time, GEODES was built from a mix of open-source technologies and proprietary solutions developed at CNES.

However, as data volumes grew and user needs evolved, GEODES's legacy infrastructure has started to become a bottleneck for rapid feature delivery. Users increasingly required better **scalability** and **interoperability** to interconnect with third-party platforms, and more transparent, well-documented interfaces. Furthermore, new, highly requested datasets, such as the very high resolution Pléiades products, as well as new services and **cloud optimized** data formats expected by the community have driven us to re-evaluate the GEODES architecture.

This is why starting in 2026 we have completely overhauled the GEODES architecture. This revamp focuses on two core pillars: **Open Source software** and **Cloud-Optimized** solutions. By moving away from proprietary silos, we have aligned the platform with modern geospatial standards improving performances and interoperability with other geomatic platforms.

Using this poster as a basis, we will discuss why and how we successfully transitioned GEODES from a monolithic, on-premises model to a highly scalable, cloud-ready, and open-source architecture. We will then detail the new architecture built on a foundation of open-source software and OGC Standards, including:

 - **OpenSearch** as the indexed catalog (https://github.com/opensearch-project)
 - **STAC Fast API** for metadata standardization and data access (https://github.com/stac-utils/stac-fastapi)
 - **OGC processes** for On-demand Processing (https://github.com/opengeospatial/ogcapi-processes)
 - **GeoServer** and **Titiler** for data display and visualization (https://github.com/geoserver/geoserver and https://github.com/developmentseed/titiler)
 - **Keycloak** for secure authentication and granular access control (https://github.com/keycloak/keycloak)
 - **Kong Gateway** for API management (https://github.com/Kong/kong)

We will also showcase the new tools and services that we made available to researchers, developers, and the general public.

Proposal title: Mapping the Drivers of Deforestation in REDD+ Project Areas across the Brazilian Amazon
Abstract: Forest loss threatens biodiversity, climate stability, and local livelihoods in the Brazilian Amazon. This study analyzes 95 forest carbon projects (~104,000 km²) using MapBiomas data integrated in GIS. Spatial analyses, mixed-effects models, and explainable AI identify socioeconomic, institutional, and landscape drivers of deforestation, guiding strategic forest management.
Description: Global forest loss remains a critical environmental issue, with tropical forests constituting a significant portion of annual deforestation. In the Brazilian Amazon, extensive forest clearing continues to threaten biodiversity, climate stability, and the livelihoods of local and Indigenous communities. In response, forest carbon initiatives like REDD+ have been created to reduce emissions from deforestation while encouraging sustainable land use and local development. Despite these efforts, some REDD+ project areas in the Brazilian Amazon still experience forest loss. This study analyzes 95 voluntary REDD+ projects covering approximately 104,000 km² implemented in the Brazilian Amazon between 2003 and 2025. We explore how socioeconomic, institutional, and landscape factors, including proximity to roads, project age, and local GDP, relate to annual deforestation rates using MapBiomas satellite data integrated in GIS platforms. Spatial analyses, correlation analyses, mixed-effects regression models, and explainable artificial intelligence techniques are used to identify drivers of forest loss at local and regional levels. Investigating the factors behind deforestation within REDD+ project areas, this research enhances understanding of the conditions that affect project effectiveness. The findings highlight key socioeconomic, institutional, and landscape drivers of forest loss and suggest pathways for improving the design of future carbon projects, promoting more strategic and spatially informed forest management in Brazil.

Proposal title: Working with NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar (NISAR) Mission Data in QGIS
Abstract: The NISAR mission launched in July 2025, and data is now publicly available. This poster presents resources and guidance for accessing and working with analysis-ready NISAR data in QGIS.
Description:
The NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar (NISAR) mission launched in July 2025, and data is now freely available to the public. SAR sensors use active microwave signals that penetrate through clouds and smoke, allowing imagery of Earth’s surface to be acquired on every pass. This reliable imaging cycle makes NISAR a powerful tool for monitoring landscape processes.  

The NISAR mission generates a number of analysis-ready SAR products for each acquisition, making it easier for non-specialists to work with SAR data and learn how to integrate these resources into new or existing GIS workflows. The Alaska Satellite Facility’s [NISAR Data User Guide](https://nisar-docs.asf.alaska.edu/) provides access to a range of resources available for working with NISAR data in QGIS.

NISAR data products are provided in an HDF5 format, but the coordinate system is encoded using CF conventions, which are designed to be used with netCDF files. This blend of file structures makes NISAR data incompatible with traditional HDF5 drivers. As such, accommodations need to be made when adding NISAR data to a QGIS project. 

This poster provides introductory content for any QGIS user interested in working with NISAR data. It highlights the most GIS-friendly NISAR data products, and provides guidance for working with them in QGIS. Topics include data access mechanisms, methods for adding NISAR HDF5 content to QGIS projects, and common approaches for visualizing and analyzing NISAR datasets in QGIS.

Proposal title: Programmatic Approaches to Exploring and Working with NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar (NISAR) Mission Datasets
Abstract: The NISAR mission launched in July 2025, and data is now publicly available. This poster presents resources and guidance for working with NISAR data programmatically using open-source packages.
Description:
The NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar (NISAR) mission launched in July 2025, and data is now freely available to the public. SAR sensors use active microwave signals that penetrate through clouds and smoke, allowing imagery of Earth’s surface to be acquired on every pass. Regular imaging cycles, combined with the ability to quantify surface deformation on the centimeter scale, make NISAR a powerful tool for monitoring landscape processes. 

The NISAR mission provides a number of Analysis-Ready Data (ARD) products that can reduce the need to perform extensive preprocessing of datasets prior to incorporating them into new and existing programmatic workflows. These products are processed to predetermined specifications, which may not be suitable for every use case.

There are a number of considerations to take into account when working with NISAR ARD products. NISAR’s HDF5 file format may be unfamiliar to users, or not be supported for some workflows. In addition, NISAR data products are very large, with some exceeding 20 GB per scene. Finally, there are several ways to access the data, and selecting the appropriate access pattern for a given situation greatly impacts workflow efficiency. 

This poster presents content from the Alaska Satellite Facility’s [NISAR Data User Guide](https://nisar-docs.asf.alaska.edu/) and a collection of Jupyter Notebook tutorials that highlight data availability, access methods, and data transformation (file format conversion, pixel spacing, reprojection, subsetting, etc.). It also features a number of processing workflows, both for generating advanced products for specific applications and for replicating some of the archived data products with custom parameters applied.

There are a number of open-source tools (isce3, gdal, xarray, rioxarray, s3fs, h5py, asf_search, earthaccess, etc.) and API-backed services (ASF HyP3, NASA Harmony, ASF Data Search) available for working with NISAR data, but it can be difficult to know which tools are most suitable for a given workflow. We highlight resources that help users determine suitable approaches for their use case.

Proposal title: Development of a Smart Offshore Aquaculture System for Seaweed Biomass Production using Open-Source Geospatial Technologies
Abstract: This talk introduces a smart offshore aquaculture system for seaweed biomass production. Built on PostgreSQL, PostGIS, and GeoServer, the open-source geospatial platform integrates environmental sensor data to overcome coastal limitations, enabling remote monitoring and establishing a foundation for carbon absorption MRV.
Description:
This study introduces a smart offshore aquaculture system developed through Korea-U.S. joint research to overcome the spatial limitations of coastal aquaculture and discover new national carbon sinks. To ensure the stable mass production of seaweed biomass in offshore environments (water depths of 35m and 200m), an integrated digital management system is essential.

At the core of this platform is a robust open-source geospatial architecture that powers our smart aquaculture management system. We leverage PostgreSQL and its spatial extension PostGIS to store and manage vast amounts of spatial data, including aquaculture facility locations, multi-depth environmental sensor data, and seaweed growth indicators. Spatial data preparation and analysis are conducted using QGIS. Furthermore, we utilize GeoServer to publish spatial data as web services and OpenLayers to implement a web-based monitoring dashboard, allowing remote control of the aquaculture environment and real-time visualization of marine conditions and mobility traces.

By integrating sensor data with open-source mapping technologies, this system not only automates growth management and harvesting but also establishes a spatial data foundation for an MRV (Measurement, Reporting, and Verification) system to quantify carbon absorption. Ultimately, the practical application of FOSS4G technologies in this study provides a scalable and sustainable framework for the marine bio-industry and offshore environments.

Keywords: Offshore aquaculture, Seaweed biomass, Smart aquaculture, Open-source GIS, PostgreSQL, PostGIS, GeoServer
Acknowledgments: This research was supported by KIMST funded by the Ministry of Oceans and Fisheries as part of the "Korea-U.S. Joint Seaweed Biomass Production System Technology Development Project" (RS-2025-02304428).

Proposal title: Big Data Transportation Planning Decision Support Using an Open-Source WebGIS Digital Twin
Abstract: This talk presents a WebGIS-based digital twin platform that integrates open-source geospatial technologies and urban mobility data to support data-driven transportation planning. The system enables scenario simulation, accessibility analysis, and policy evaluation through an extensible open-source architecture.
Description:
Although urban digital twin platforms are increasingly adopted worldwide, many implementations remain focused on 3D visualization and monitoring, with limited integration of scenario-based analysis and decision support within an open WebGIS architecture.

This presentation introduces a big data–driven transportation planning decision support system built on an open-source WebGIS-based digital twin platform. The system integrates smart card data, floating population data, and multimodal transport networks into a unified spatial database.

The architecture consists of modular components including data ingestion, spatial database integration, simulation engine, visualization services, and reporting modules. Scenario-based analysis enables planners to evaluate potential policy alternatives such as new bus stop placement, route restructuring, accessibility improvement, and late-night bus introduction.

Results are visualized in both 2D and 3D environments with temporal playback, before-after comparison, and interactive layer controls. Key performance indicators such as accessibility, service efficiency, and equity can be automatically generated.

The platform demonstrates how open-source geospatial technologies can support practical digital twin implementations for transportation planning. The talk will share system architecture, integration workflows, and lessons learned from pilot implementations.

Proposal title: Web-Based High-Performance Traffic Simulator: Advancing an Integrated Interaction Platform for Urban Planning Decision-Making
Abstract: Building on previous research, we introduce a traffic simulation platform enhanced for real-world urban planning. By optimizing large-scale data processing and interactions, we demonstrate technical maturity as a practical decision-support tool, moving beyond visualization toward immediate, high-performance deployment in professional and production-ready environments.
Description:
Based on the 2D/3D visualization and scenario editing workflows established in previous years, the third year focuses on securing PERFORMANCE EXCELLENCE and OPERATIONAL EFFICIENCY required for professional environments.

[Key Advancements]
• WebGL-based High-Performance Engine and State Management Optimization
: Optimized the WebGL rendering engine to process large-scale traffic networks and high-precision terrain data without latency. Designed a sophisticated State Management structure between Cesium and OpenLayers to ensure stable operation.

• User-Centric Interaction Enhancement
: Developed an interface to precisely control complex road structures, such as overpasses and underpasses. Improved UX to reflect professional workflows, increasing the convenience of simulation settings.

• Flexible Data Compatibility
: Strengthened Import/Export functionalities for seamless integration with external simulation engines and various GIS data formats, maximizing scalability and practical data utility.

• Analytical Reporting System
: Added a dashboard-based reporting feature to provide intuitive insights. This allows analytical data to be immediately utilized as evidence for urban policy-making.

Technical Stack & Standards
• 3D Geospatial Visualization: CesiumJS
(Immersive 3D visualization, terrain rendering, and dynamic movement animation)
• 2D Vector Rendering: OpenLayers
(High-performance engine for WebGL-based 2D vector rendering)
• Spatial Database: PostGIS / PostgreSQL
(Robust spatial data storage, advanced indexing, and topological analysis)

[Conclusion]
In this presentation, we share our experience in building a PRODUCTION-READY PLATFORM that implements professional-grade functionality using open-source technologies, designed for IMMEDIATE DEPLOYMENT in actual urban planning processes.

[Acknowledgment]
This work was supported by Institute of Information & communications Technology Planning & Evaluation (IITP) grant funded by the Korea government(MSIT) (RS-2024-00459703, Development of next-generation AI integrated mobility simulation and prediction/application technologies for metropolitan cities)

Proposal title: USE-SVI: A Reproducible Pipeline for Sampling and Processing Street-Level Imagery for Urban Analytics
Abstract: USE-SVI is a Python pipeline for systematically sampling road networks, acquiring Google Street View images, and stitching them into panoramas. It produces standardized geospatial outputs that support reproducible street-level imagery datasets for urban analytics and machine learning applications.
Description:
Street-level imagery (SLI) has become an important data source for urban analytics, supporting applications such as estimating urban greenery, evaluating walkability and transport infrastructure, and assessing building facades. However, research using SLI often suffers from inconsistent image quality, irregular spatial coverage, and non-standardized acquisition procedures, which limit the reproducibility and comparability of results across studies and cities.

To address these challenges, we developed USE-SVI (Urban Sampling & Extraction of Street View Imagery), a reproducible Python-based pipeline designed to systematically sample, acquire, and process street-level images for city-scale analysis. The method generates sampling points along the road network at fixed spatial intervals, ensuring regular coverage of the urban environment. For each sampling location, four viewing directions (0°, 90°, 180°, and 270°) are generated to capture the main visual perspectives of the street environment.

Image acquisition is automated through browser-based interaction with the Google Street View web interface using Selenium WebDriver, enabling systematic retrieval of street-view images without relying on official APIs. The pipeline is implemented in Python and relies on common geospatial and data-processing libraries such as GeoPandas, Shapely, and Pandas for spatial sampling and metadata handling, while OpenCV is used for image processing and panorama stitching. During acquisition, relevant metadata (e.g., coordinates, orientation, and image identifiers) are recorded to ensure traceability and reproducibility of the dataset.

Downloaded images are subsequently stitched into panoramas using OpenCV-based feature matching and image blending procedures, producing unified street-view scenes that better represent each sampling location. The pipeline exports standardized outputs, including images and structured metadata tables (CSV/XLSX), that can be readily integrated into GIS environments or used as input for computer vision and machine learning workflows.

By formalizing the stages of spatial sampling, automated acquisition, and image stitching, USE-SVI provides a transparent and reproducible workflow for generating consistent SLI datasets across cities and time periods. Limitations mainly relate to the quality of the input road network geometry used to generate sampling points and occasional panorama stitching failures in visually homogeneous scenes. The pipeline and documentation are openly available to support reuse and further development by the research community.

Proposal title: Japanese Map Culture: Inheritance through FOSS4G Technology
Abstract: Japanese map culture has evolved through a pursuit of intuitive map designs as sophisticated infographics. We are redefining this cultural legacy within the digital space using FOSS4G technology to ensure its inheritance and evolution for the next generation.
Description:
During the 1990s, the Japanese map industry developed unique designs to intuitively convey information within limited spaces. By perfecting techniques such as purposeful exaggeration, simplification, and strategic color schemes—treating the map as sophisticated infographics—a distinct Japanese map culture was established.
MAPPLE is now redefining traditional cartography in the digital realm, using FOSS4G as our technological foundation. Maps possess the power to solve social issues; we aim to unlock their inherent "intuitive clarity" through digital means, linking their potential to a better future. Furthermore, we will actively contribute to the FOSS4G community through these initiatives.

Proposal title: Utilizing detailed geographic information open data
Abstract: In Japan, open data of detailed geographic information is made public, and we will propose ways to effectively utilize these data, using the analysis of water catchment areas behind collapsed lands as an example.
Description: In Japan, local governments and the Geospatial Information Authority of Japan publish detailed geographic information open data. These data are provided at various levels of detail. When using QGIS's watershed analysis function to analyze water catchment areas  behind collapsed lands , which is a predisposing factor for collapse, using data with different levels of detail, different results can be obtained with each data. Based on this case study, we will propose ways to utilize detailed geographic information open data.

Proposal title: Automated Neighborhood-Scale Zone Delineation from Administrative Districts for Hyperlocal Applications
Abstract: This study presents a methodology for subdividing administrative districts into finer-grained local units by leveraging geographic boundary features. In contrast to uniform geometric partitioning or manually curated boundary datasets, the proposed approach minimizes human intervention while maintaining spatial coherence, offering a scalable solution for hyperlocal service area delineation.
Description:
Hyperlocal platforms rely on precisely delineated geographic service zones to facilitate community interaction within proximity-based contexts. While administrative districts serve as a conventional geographic reference, their boundaries frequently lack the granularity necessary for genuinely hyperlocal applications. This study describes an experimental methodology developed at Karrot — a hyperlocal platform by Daangn offering second-hand trading and local community services — to systematically subdivide administrative units into smaller, more contextually coherent sub-regions.
Existing spatial subdivision methods fall into two paradigms. The first encompasses uniform geometric partitioning systems such as H3 and S2, which discretize space into fixed-size cells at consistent intervals regardless of underlying geographic or socio-spatial context. While computationally efficient, these systems produce boundaries that are spatially arbitrary and fail to reflect how communities are naturally structured. The second paradigm relies on administratively or institutionally defined boundaries, such as administrative districts or postal code zones. Although these units inherently encode human activity patterns and are widely recognized, they are typically delineated at scales too coarse to serve hyperlocal service requirements.
The proposed methodology employs natural geographic features — road networks, hydrological features, railway infrastructure, and topographic elements — as structural delineation constraints when partitioning administrative districts. This approach is premised on the hypothesis that boundaries defined by salient physical features more closely align with residents' cognitive maps of their local environment. The implementation integrates GIS spatial analysis applied to open geographic datasets, encompassing three stages: preprocessing and topological harmonization of multi-source data, automated polygon construction from linear boundary features, and quality assessment against minimum area thresholds and spatial coherence criteria.

Proposal title: Strengthening Community Resilience in Dili Through Integrated Waste Mapping, Mobile-Based Reporting, and Drone-Assisted Early Warning
Abstract: Urban flooding in Dili, Timor-Leste is often worsened by unmanaged waste blocking drainage systems. This project integrates participatory mapping using OpenStreetMap, real-time community reporting through Ushahidi, and drone mapping to monitor waste hotspots and support early warning efforts, strengthening community-driven disaster risk reduction and environmental monitoring.
Description:
Rapid urban growth and inadequate waste management have increased environmental risks in Dili, Timor-Leste. Waste accumulation frequently blocks drainage systems, contributing to localized flooding during heavy rainfall. These challenges highlight the need for community-centered approaches that combine local knowledge with geospatial technologies to improve environmental monitoring and disaster preparedness.
This initiative focuses on strengthening community resilience through an integrated approach that combines participatory mapping, mobile-based reporting, and drone-assisted environmental monitoring. Community members and volunteers collaborated to identify and map waste hotspots, drainage infrastructure, and flood-prone areas using open-source geospatial platforms such as OpenStreetMap and QGIS. Through mapping workshops and field surveys, participants contributed local knowledge to produce spatial data that better reflects real conditions on the ground.
To support continuous monitoring and community engagement, a mobile reporting platform based on Ushahidi was introduced. This system enables residents to report waste accumulation, drainage blockages, and flood incidents in real time. The reports are automatically mapped and can support local authorities, community leaders, and organizations in identifying priority areas that require immediate attention or preventive action.
Drone mapping was also integrated into the initiative to provide high-resolution imagery for environmental monitoring and participatory flood risk mapping. Drone data helps visualize waste accumulation patterns, drainage pathways, and vulnerable locations that may not be easily captured through ground-based observations alone. The combination of drone imagery with community-generated data improves the accuracy and usefulness of local environmental information.
The poster presentation will highlight the methodology, tools, and community engagement processes used in this initiative, as well as key insights gained from implementing the approach. It demonstrates how open-source geospatial technologies can empower communities to actively participate in environmental monitoring, improve waste management awareness, and contribute to early warning efforts for flood risks in Timor-Leste.

Proposal title: GeoNode as a Foundation for Research Data Management Infrastructure
Abstract: This work presents the adaptation of GeoNode into a FAIR-compliant research data management (RDM) infrastructure for agricultural science. By integrating repository workflows, interactive exploration, extended metadata, and scalable Kubernetes deployment, the BonaRes Repository enables researchers to publish, discover, and reuse diverse datasets.
Description:
The growing complexity and volume of digital research data in agricultural and environmental sciences demand integrated, FAIR-compliant research data management (RDM) infrastructures. This work demonstrates how GeoNode—originally a geospatial data platform—can be extended into a comprehensive RDM system tailored to the needs of agricultural research. The result is the BonaRes Repository, a national-scale infrastructure developed at the Leibniz Centre for Agricultural Landscape Research (ZALF), now part of Germany’s FairAgro initiative, which aims to build a sustainable, FAIR data ecosystem for agricultural science.

The platform hosts over 1,000 datasets, including geospatial maps, soil profiles, experimental measurements, and model outputs. To bridge the gap between traditional repositories—strong in metadata and archiving but weak in exploration—and geospatial platforms—rich in visualization but lacking in RDM workflows—we integrated repository functionalities (persistent identifiers, structured workflows, ORCID) with interactive data exploration capabilities. Users can now inspect both spatial and non-spatial datasets directly in the browser, gaining immediate insight into data context and structure—without downloading.

Key architectural innovations include:
* Extended metadata models: Integration of DataCite, ARC (NFDI4Plants), and the BonaRes Metadata Schema (INSPIRE-aligned), enabling rich, domain-specific descriptions.
* Automated data ingestion: A custom upload tool (https://github.com/zalf-rdm/upload-tool) with Frictionless Data validation and a data steward review process, ensuring high data quality.
* Programmatic access: geonodectl (https://github.com/GeoNodeUserGroup-DE/geonodectl), a Python client for the GeoNode API, enabling automation and integration with external workflows.
* Non-geospatial support: Native handling of tabular datasets via a Tabular Collection—a tabbed interface for seamless exploration of experimental data.
* User-centric UI: Streamlined interface with hidden expert features, improving accessibility for non-GIS researchers.

For scalability and reliability, the system runs on Kubernetes using the geonode-k8s Helm chart (https://github.com/GeoNodeUserGroup-DE/geonode-k8s), enabling dynamic scaling, automated backups, and cloud-ready deployment.

This project demonstrates that GeoNode, when extended with domain-specific components, can serve as a powerful, open-source foundation for RDM. It offers a replicable blueprint for other research communities seeking to build FAIR, interoperable, and user-friendly data infrastructures—proving that open geospatial platforms can evolve into full-fledged research data ecosystems.

Proposal title: Web-Based Urban Thermal Fluid Simulation System Using 3D City Model
Abstract: We developed a system that utilizes the open-source 3D city model "PLATEAU" to enable simple thermal fluid simulations on a web application. This contributes to creating more comfortable urban environments by considering factors such as building wind flows and heat islands during urban planning.
Description:
The Web-Based Urban Thermal Fluid Simulation System, developed as part of the PLATEAU project (https://www.mlit.go.jp/plateau/en/), is an innovative tool that uses 3D city models to evaluate urban thermal environments. Unlike traditional city-scale CFD simulations requiring high-performance computers and specialized systems, this cloud-based platform enables municipal staff and non-engineers to run and visualize large-scale simulations directly via a standard web browser, removing dependence on local PC specifications.

Global warming has increased concerns about the urban heat island effect, highlighting the need for quantitative evaluation of environmental measures. Using this simulator, urban planners can assess the impact of interventions like rooftop and wall greening, water sprinkling (uchimizu), new high-rise construction, and vegetation development. These assessments can inform decision-making before actual implementation, promoting Evidence-Based Policy Making (EBPM).

The core thermal analysis engine uses "OpenFOAM," an open-source CFD software, to compute factors such as solar radiation, wind speed, and temperature. The web application features four main functions:

1. 3D City Model Editing: Users can add or remove trees and buildings on the 3D map intuitively for analysis.

2. Visualization: Using WebGIS tools like CesiumJS, the system visualizes 3D city models (LOD1) and simulation results, including wind flow, temperature, and heat maps like Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT). These metrics are displayed intuitively for easy interpretation.

3. Thermal Fluid Analysis: Before simulations, users can set environmental parameters including wind speed, direction, humidity, temperature, date/time, and heat mitigation measures.

4. Publishing: Simulation results can be shared via a dedicated URL, allowing residents and stakeholders without accounts to view and understand the analysis.

A key feature is its user-friendly graphical interface. Users can perform operations such as adding or deleting buildings directly on the web map, enabling side-by-side comparison of "before and after" urban redevelopment and heat island countermeasures. Demonstrations in Yokosuka, Saitama, and Kumagaya cities received praise from officials for usability and functionality. The ability to share large-scale redevelopment images in 3D greatly enhances communication and consensus-building with communities.

This system effectively integrates advanced simulation tools into urban management workflows, supporting smarter, more sustainable city development.
The source code and documentation are publicly available(Japanese):
https://github.com/Project-PLATEAU/Fluid-dynamics-simulator
https://www.mlit.go.jp/plateau/use-case/uc23-25/
https://www.mlit.go.jp/plateau/use-case/uc24-04/

Proposal title: Creating Language to Describe a Subjective Geospatial World: Development of a System for the Dynamic Generation of Mental Maps
Abstract: It is well known that human behavior often follows mental maps that differ from the actual layout of the physical environment. We propose a system that quantifies these ``discrepancies'' in individual spatial cognition and converts objective GIS data into subjective mental maps.
Description:
1. Background and Motivation for Development
Conventional GIS systems have primarily focused on handling data for geographic features that are precisely positioned based on surveying. However, real-world human behavior does not necessarily follow such objective maps. It is well known that everyday spatial behaviors, such as walking and route selection, are guided by mental maps—that is, mental representations that diverge from the actual physical layout—due to individual experience and cognitive biases. This system views this discrepancy as important data reflecting the characteristics of individual spatial cognition and aims to build a technological foundation for reconstructing objective information into an individual's subjective geospatial world.

2. Theoretical Basis of Mental Maps
According to prior research in human geography, certain patterns have been reported regarding discrepancies in mental maps. For example, these include a tendency to overestimate or underestimate the distance between known locations compared to the physical distance, as well as the ``rotation heuristic'', in which the overall orientation is simplified and rotated based on major road networks (paths) for memory retention. This system adopts these phenomena as input parameters for quantification.

3. System Implementation Flow
This system consists of two phases: an attempt to extract and quantify the user's spatial cognitive characteristics, and a phase that converts objective maps into mental maps based on the quantified data.
First, the user plots landmarks and major features within a specific district on a blank canvas within the system, relying solely on their memory without referring to an actual map. The system cross-references this subjective plotting data with the corresponding actual coordinates and extracts the differences. This allows the system to calculate an individual's spatial cognition characteristics as geometric features.
Next, based on the calculated features, objective GIS data—such as buildings and roads—is dynamically distorted to fit the user's unique spatial perception, enabling the visualization and rendering of the mental map.

4. Challenges and Dreams
Mental maps are influenced by an individual's actual behavioral patterns and emotions within the city. In other words, it is necessary to verify the validity of the quantified data by excluding parameters that are difficult for this system to capture.
By quantifying spatial cognitive tendencies and treating them as data formats analyzable via GIS, applications beyond mere visualization are anticipated. By analyzing the trends in residents' mental maps for any given district based on accumulated parameters, we anticipate applications in disaster prevention, such as assessing awareness of evacuation shelters and understanding or even predicting route selection during evacuations. Furthermore, mental maps are believed to encompass individual behavior and emotions. We would be delighted if this system could serve as a catalyst for communication through mental maps and help foster a sense of attachment to places.

(As of March 2026, this project is in the conceptual stage.)

Proposal title: Soybean yield estimation using free geotechnologies: modeling with remote sensing and Random Forest
Abstract: This study estimated soybean yield using NDVI and EVI derived from MODIS and Sentinel-2 sensors. Using free geotechnologies, the Random Forest algorithm was applied for modeling. The comparison between observed and estimated data indicated good performance, with higher accuracy achieved using Sentinel-2 data.
Description:
The use of free and open geotechnologies has expanded access to advanced tools for spatial analysis and agricultural monitoring, enabling the development of efficient and accessible solutions. In this context, this study aimed to estimate soybean yield through the integration of remote sensing and modeling using the Random Forest algorithm, based entirely on open technologies.
The research was conducted in 16 agricultural plots located in the southwestern region of Paraná State, Brazil, during the 2020/2021 growing season. Actual yield data were obtained using a yield monitor, allowing the validation of the generated models. For yield estimation, the vegetation indices NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) and EVI (Enhanced Vegetation Index) were used, derived from MODIS (Terra and Aqua satellites) and MSI (Sentinel-2) imagery.
The workflow involved processing satellite images, extracting vegetation indices, and organizing spatial data, followed by modeling with the Random Forest algorithm. This approach enabled handling large datasets and capturing nonlinear relationships between spectral variables and crop yield.
The results showed good performance for both sensors. The Sentinel-2-based model presented a coefficient of determination (R²) of approximately 0.60 and a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.50, while the MODIS-based model showed an R² of 0.63 and RMSE of 0.59. The comparison between observed yield data and model estimates demonstrated higher accuracy for Sentinel-2, with an average difference of approximately 4 kg ha⁻¹, compared to about 145 kg ha⁻¹ for MODIS.
Additionally, vegetation indices obtained in January and February, corresponding to the final stages of crop development, were identified as the most relevant predictors of yield. The results highlight the potential of integrating free geotechnologies, remote sensing, and Random Forest for precision agriculture applications, supporting crop monitoring and decision-making at regional scales.

Proposal title: Construction of a Geospatial Digital Archive of the Fukui Air Raid History
Abstract: This presentation introduces the Fukui Air Raid Digital Archive, a community-based project using open-source GIS to map war memories, historical records, and disaster impacts, supporting education, cultural heritage preservation, and spatial understanding of post-war reconstruction.
Description:
The project visualizes damage from the July 1945 Fukui air raid by digitizing historical records, particularly victim data from The History of the Fukui Air Raid (1978). Approximately 830 victim records were digitized, with 752 locations geocoded. Historical maps were georeferenced and integrated into a GIS environment using Re:Earth, an open-source 3D GIS platform enabling public WebGIS access.

Analysis reveals damage concentration within 1-1.5 km of the city center, with casualty distribution showing multiple peaks at specific distance bands (100-200 m, 500-600 m, 700-800 m), indicating complex relationships between urban structure and fire spread.

The project employed community-based implementation, with university students collaborating with regional newspaper companies and local institutions. This approach preserved historical memory while providing educational opportunities in GIS and digital archiving.

By transforming analog records into interactive geospatial data, the project deepens understanding of localized damage patterns and demonstrates open-source technologies' potential for cultural heritage preservation and disaster education.

Proposal title: Sketch and Stats: Integrating Community Mapping into Spatial Verification of Flood Extents
Abstract: A spatial approach to enhance flood extent verification by integrating community mapping with hydrodynamic simulation outputs. The method provides a structured framework to compare and interpret flood maps using spatial agreement analysis.
Description:
One of the common practices in hydrodynamic flood modelling in Malaysia is to verify simulated flood extents against real-world observations, typically by consulting affected residents to confirm whether flooding occurred at selected locations and approximate water depths. These verification outcomes are then documented in flood reports submitted to authorities. However, such approaches are often based on limited point-based observations and qualitative confirmation, which may not fully represent the spatial extent of flooding. This introduces challenges in relating discrete verification points to continuous flood extent maps.

This paper presents a spatially explicit approach to support flood extent verification by integrating community-derived flood observations with simulated flood extents. Spatial flood perception was collected through sketch mapping of the December 2021 flood event in the Damansara rivee catchment, involving 417 respondents across three neighbourhoods: Kampung (village) Melayu Subang (117), TTDI Jaya (182), and Kampung (village) Melayu Kebun Bunga (118). The sketches were aggregated and transformed into a gridded perception surface using a proportion-based threshold, and subsequently compared with a two-dimensional hydrodynamic flood extent using a cell-based classification framework. The workflow was implemented using open-source GIS tools, ensuring transparency and reproducibility.

The comparison produced three spatial outcomes: shared flooded areas, perceived-only areas, and simulated-only areas, which were further quantified using Recall, Precision, and Jaccard similarity metrics. Results indicate varying degrees of spatial correspondence across neighbourhoods, with strong agreement observed in Kg. Melayu Kebun Bunga and greater divergence in TTDI Jaya and Kg. Melayu Subang. These differences reflect how simulated flood extents and community-reported experiences may capture different aspects of the same flood event, particularly where localised or drainage-related flooding may not be represented in the simulation.

Beyond comparison, the approach demonstrates how community-derived inputs can be systematically translated into spatial evidence for flood extent verification. By moving from point-based confirmation to area-based comparison, the method provides a more spatially grounded way to interpret model performance. Rather than treating simulated and perceived extents as competing representations, the workflow bridges both by highlighting areas of agreement and divergence, supporting a more comprehensive understanding of flood behaviour during real events.

The proposed method complements existing verification practices by offering a structured and reproducible way to incorporate community knowledge into flood modelling workflows. With appropriate adaptation to sampling design and data collection, this approach can support improved spatial verification of flood maps and contribute to more informed flood risk assessment and disaster risk reduction efforts.

Proposal title: Contributing to Landslide Disaster Prevention Measures in the Noto Region Using Open Source Software and Open Data
Abstract: The 2024 Noto Peninsula Earthquake triggered landslides and heightened sediment disaster risk, worsened by September 2024 heavy rainfall. Using open geospatial data, the authors identified "hidden landslides" via template matching and assessed post-earthquake slope instability. A web platform was developed to visualize topographic changes for disaster prevention.
Description:
The 2024 Noto Peninsula Earthquake of January 2024 triggered numerous landslides across the northern Noto Peninsula. Subsequently, heavy rainfall in September 2024 caused further landslides and erosion on slopes already weakened by the earthquake, amplifying flood damage in downstream areas. Since the January earthquake, the northern Noto Peninsula has remained in a state of heightened susceptibility to rainfall-induced sediment disasters compared to pre-earthquake conditions.

In parallel, this disaster event prompted the release of substantial geospatial information as open data from both public and private sectors. In particular, pre- and post-earthquake DEM and point cloud datasets covering the entire northern Noto Peninsula became available, enabling detailed assessment of sediment disaster occurrences.

The authors utilized these open datasets to investigate the mechanisms of earthquake-triggered landslides and to evaluate the risk of rainfall-induced sediment disasters in the post-earthquake period. Template matching analysis of pre- and post-earthquake DEMs revealed that the January earthquake produced numerous landslides best described as "hidden landslides" — events involving ground displacements of several meters but without the formation of new bare ground surfaces. Analysis of factors influencing slope failures during the September 2024 heavy rainfall further demonstrated that earthquake-induced landslides and gravitational creep deformation had an extremely significant effect on slope destabilization.

The distribution of unstable sediment deposits and gravitationally deformed slopes resulting from the earthquake can be readily identified using topographic change data published by the Japan Forestry Agency, which is provided in the form of PNG elevation tiles. The authors developed a web platform to assist municipal staff and private-sector disaster-prevention engineers in viewing this earthquake-induced topographic change data and sharing information on hazard locations.

Proposal title: Small Robot Mapping Using Local Mesh Communication and Open-Source Sensors
Abstract: This project presents a low-cost educational multi-robot mapping prototype using ESP32 microcontrollers, open-source sensors, and local mesh communication, where mobile robots exchange sensor observations and relative positions to collaboratively generate a shared occupancy-grid map without relying on external network infrastructure.
Description:
This project for poster presents an educational prototype for multi-robot mapping using low-cost open-source hardware and decentralized communication. Unlike many robotics systems that depend on central servers or external Wi-Fi infrastructure, the prototype explores a fully local approach in which simple mobile robots communicate directly through a mesh network to build a shared map of their environment.

Each robot is built around an ESP32 and equipped with basic sensors, including ultrasonic distance sensors for obstacle detection and motion-based estimation for relative positioning. As robots move, they exchange local coordinates, sensor readings, and obstacle detections in real time with neighboring units through peer-to-peer communication, allowing operation entirely offline without a central router.

Shared observations from multiple robots are integrated into a two-dimensional occupancy grid representing nearby obstacles and relative robot locations within a common local coordinate frame. This cooperative sensing approach extends spatial awareness beyond the capability of a single unit and demonstrates practical concepts in distributed sensing and collaborative mapping.

The prototype is intended as a hands-on educational platform that introduces geospatial thinking, decentralized spatial data exchange, and multi-agent coordination through accessible open-source embedded systems and mesh-based communication.

Proposal title: Yagō Audio Map in Karakuwa Peninsula
Abstract: We developed "Yagō Audio Map in Karakuwa Peninsula" with Leaflet to preserve local culture.
Description:
In recent years, there has been growing attention to the use of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for the preservation and revitalization of local culture in Japan. This momentum is driven by the nationwide expansion of cultural heritage management policies, the rapid development of digital archiving and geospatial platforms, and the urgent need to document cultural practices threatened by depopulation and aging communities. GIS has become a key for integrating spatial information, community memory, and cultural narratives in ways that support both conservation and regional revitalization.
This study introduces a web-based “Yagō Audio Map” developed collaboratively by the voluntary research group GLOCAL Research Association and the local town planning council. The project aims to preserve orally transmitted cultural knowledge by documenting the traditional house names (yagō) of the Karakuwa Peninsula in Tohoku, Japan. Yagō are long-standing, non-official house names historically used in farming and fishing communities to distinguish households, kinship lines, and social roles—often more meaningful locally than legal surnames. In Karakuwa, yagō remain an essential part of everyday communication, especially for older residents. Many of these names derive from natural features, directions, or landscape elements, reflecting the intimate relationship between community life and the local environment.
The project recorded the yagō of all 1,821 households across 12 districts, with three local residents providing the audio pronunciations. The digital audio map was created using Leaflet (an open-source JavaScript library for WebGIS) based on information compiled in a 2003 “Yagō Telephone Directory” produced by the town’s Chamber of Commerce youth group. By clicking on a household on a tablet-based map, users can view its yagō and hear its spoken form, preserving pronunciation, nuance, and oral tradition that text alone cannot convey. In fact, this audio map is compiled as a local application on display at the visitor center of the Karakuwa Peninsula, cherished by visitors and residents.
By combining geospatial visualization with community-recorded oral histories, the Yagō Audio Map demonstrates how GIS-based platforms can support community-led cultural preservation. The project highlights the broader potential of GIS to serve as an interface between intangible cultural heritage, local knowledge, and contemporary digital practices at a moment when safeguarding regional culture has become increasingly urgent.

Proposal title: Point Cloud PNG: Efficient Web-Based Distribution of Large-Scale Point Cloud Data via Image Encoding
Abstract: Point Cloud PNG is a file format for publishing two- or three-dimensional positional data using PNG/WebP with lossless compression. It encodes numerical data as pixels, enabling efficient storage, GPU-based processing, and substantial reduction of large point cloud datasets.
Description:
The Geological Survey of Japan released the official version of Point Cloud PNG (ver. 1.0) free of charge on October 1, 2025. Point Cloud PNG is a file format designed to publish datasets with two- or three-dimensional positional information over the Internet using image file formats such as PNG or WebP. PNG and WebP support lossless compression, which enables reduction of file size and makes them suitable for data transfer over the Internet. In addition, image data can be efficiently processed using GPUs. We refer to this concept of high compression of data using image files as “Data PNG,” not limited to Point Cloud PNG.
In Point Cloud PNG, numerical values are converted into pixels and stored in an image file. A Point Cloud PNG image does not carry visual meaning as an image; rather, it uses the image file as a container for storing data based on pixel arrays. The basic structure involves converting header information, X coordinate arrays, and Y coordinate arrays into colors and arranging them in raster scan order starting from the top-left corner of the image. In addition, Z coordinate arrays and user-defined attribute data arrays can be appended.
When handling large-scale point cloud data, it is effective to combine this approach with map tiling. Data divided in the same manner as map tiles used in two-dimensional web maps are referred to as Point Cloud PNG tiles.
On the Point Cloud PNG publication page, an example of two-dimensional Point Cloud PNG is presented, in which point data from rapid survey maps are converted into Point Cloud PNG. Converting the original GeoJSON file (approximately 381 MB) into Point Cloud PNG reduced the file size to approximately 833 KB, which corresponds to about 0.22% (1/457) of the original size. In addition, in a Point Cloud PNG tile set created for the area around the summit of Mount Fuji and the Hoei crater, it was confirmed that a LAS file set of 25.6 GB could be compressed to approximately 10.9 GB (about 42%).

Proposal title: (Academic track) Leveraging Geospatial Big Data for Smart City Digital Twins: A Framework for 3D Modeling and Solar Energy Assessment
Abstract: A rigorous, fully open-source spatial framework developed within QGIS for generating smart city digital twins using high-density airborne LiDAR data to assess rooftop photovoltaic potential in urban environments.
Description:
This research addresses the limitations of two-dimensional building models in assessing solar photovoltaic capacity by presenting a comprehensive open-source methodology. The study utilizes high-density airborne LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) point cloud data from the UK Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs to create detailed 3D city models in a Birmingham case study.

The workflow extracts 2D building footprints, generates Digital Terrain and Surface Models, and creates Normalized Digital Surface Models for absolute building heights. Rather than applying smoothing algorithms that distort authentic roof inclinations, the framework preserves raw LiDAR topological characteristics. Solar radiation is computed using SAGA GIS with ray-tracing and viewshed analysis to account for urban micro-shading effects.

The methodology segments buildings into distinct roof planes by reclassifying aspect layers into cardinal directions and filtering for viable installation areas. A conservative 0.60 usability coefficient translates gross solar radiation into realistic energy yield projections. The final output provides building-by-building spatial inventories within QGIS showing total roof area, optimally oriented installable area, and potential annual energy generation in kWh. Future work will integrate Energy Performance Certificate data to enable supply-demand matching analysis for urban energy autonomy assessment.

Proposal title: (Academic track) Open-Source Geospatial Workflow for Hydro-Morphological Vulnerability Modelling in Low-Lying Deltaic Systems: Application to the Core Niger Delta, Nigeria
Abstract: A reproducible hydro-morphological vulnerability modelling workflow implemented using free and open-source geospatial technologies, demonstrated in the Core Niger Delta of Nigeria.
Description:
Low-lying deltaic environments face significant environmental vulnerabilities including flooding, sediment instability, and coastal hazards from sea-level rise. This study presents a systematic approach integrating terrain analysis and drainage morphometry within a unified FOSS4G modelling framework. Rather than relying on proprietary geographic information systems, the workflow uses QGIS, GRASS GIS, and SAGA GIS to support transparency and reproducibility.

The methodology employs SRTM 30-meter digital elevation data as the primary topographic dataset. Hydrological conditioning removes spurious depressions to ensure consistent flow routing. Key analyses include slope gradient derivation, flow direction and accumulation computation, stream network extraction, and drainage density calculation using grid-based methods. Surface moisture potential is represented through the Topographic Wetness Index, combining slope and upslope contributing area.

Six hydro-morphological indicators are derived: elevation, slope, flow accumulation, topographic wetness index, drainage density, and distance to rivers. All variables are standardized using min-max normalization to 0-1 ranges, then integrated into a composite Hydro-Morphological Vulnerability Index using equal-weight linear combination. Results are classified into five vulnerability categories from Very Low to Very High.

The Core Niger Delta case study demonstrates how terrain-hydrology interactions increase vulnerability in low-elevation floodplains and intertidal swamp belts. Model performance will be evaluated through spatial comparison with documented flood-prone locations and sensitivity analysis varying indicator weights. This approach supports climate resilience planning and demonstrates FOSS4G's capacity for rigorous geospatial research applicable to other vulnerable deltaic regions.
